sections.title: Global Equities
sections.content: Global equities rebounded as fears of a wider Middle East conflict faded, oil retreated to pre-war levels and a soft US jobs report tempered rate-hike concerns. MSCI World rose roughly **1.7%** (approx., verify before merging), MSCI Developed Markets about **1.7%** (approx., verify before merging) and MSCI Emerging Markets around **1.2%** (approx., verify before merging). The S&P 500 gained about **1.8%** to a record **7,483**, while the MAG7 mega-cap cohort ended broadly flat to modestly higher after a volatile, chip-led week (approx., verify before merging). Europe's Euro Stoxx 50 climbed about **2.3%** to near **6,417**. In Asia the Nikkei 225 advanced **3.14%**, China's CSI 300 rose around **1%** (approx., verify before merging) and Hong Kong's Hang Seng added roughly **1%** (approx., verify before merging).

sections.title: US Equities
sections.content:
US indices rallied to fresh records in a holiday-shortened week, with markets closed on Friday for Independence Day. The S&P 500 rose about **1.8%** to a record close near **7,483**, the Dow gained roughly **2%** to a record **52,900**, and the Nasdaq Composite added about **2.1%** despite a Thursday dip of **0.8%** as mega-cap tech and AI names swung sharply.

Sentiment was lifted by de-escalation in the Middle East, a slide in oil prices, and easing rate-hike fears after Fed Chair Warsh signalled that inflation risks had receded alongside lower energy costs. A rotation into cyclicals and industrials powered the Dow to its record.

Macro data softened. June nonfarm payrolls rose just **57,000**, well below the roughly **115,000** consensus and May's pace, while unemployment ticked down to **4.2%**. The cooler labour print reinforced expectations that the Fed will hold rates for the remainder of 2026 rather than tighten.

sections.title: European Equities
sections.content:
European equities outperformed as hopes of a Middle East de-escalation and falling oil prices buoyed risk appetite. The pan-European STOXX Europe 600 rose **3.0%** in local-currency terms, while the Euro Stoxx 50 gained about **2.3%** to near **6,417**, with broad participation across the region.

Germany's export-heavy DAX led the majors, up **3.92%**, followed by France's CAC 40 at **2.05%** and Italy's FTSE MIB at **0.80%**. Cyclicals, autos and industrials rebounded as energy-cost pressures eased and the growth outlook steadied.

The macro backdrop turned more supportive: eurozone headline CPI fell to **2.8%** in June from **3.2%** in May, below the **3%** consensus, bolstering the case for further ECB easing. Softer inflation and cheaper oil offered relief to the region's net importers and underpinned the rally into the close.

sections.title: APAC Equities
sections.content:
APAC markets recovered as the global mood improved and regional technology names stabilised after a mid-week chip-led sell-off. Japan's Nikkei 225 rose **3.14%** and the broader TOPIX gained **0.74%**, supported by a weaker yen and receding geopolitical risk, though semiconductor and AI-linked shares remained volatile through the week.

Chinese equities firmed into Friday, with Hong Kong's Hang Seng up **1.28%** and the mainland CSI 300 rising **1.15%** on the day, as investors continued to await fresh domestic stimulus signals. Asia ex-Japan broadly tracked the steadier tone in global mega-cap technology.

Lower oil prices offered an additional tailwind for the region's energy importers, while easing US rate-hike fears and a softening dollar supported sentiment across Asian risk assets into the holiday-thinned close.

sections.title: Foreign Exchange
sections.content:
The US dollar weakened, with the DXY index down about **0.6%** on the week to its lowest since mid-June, as a downside surprise in June payrolls trimmed expectations for further Fed tightening. The move partly unwound earlier dollar strength built on higher-for-longer rate pricing and a widening G10 yield advantage.

Sterling was among the strongest majors, reaching one-year highs against the euro, while the euro lagged after June inflation printed below expectations, reinforcing ECB easing bets. Risk-friendly currencies benefited from the calmer geopolitical backdrop.

The yen stayed under pressure despite the softer dollar: USD/JPY touched **162.84** on 1 July, its highest since December 1986, before easing after the jobs data. With the pair near four-decade lows, the risk of official Japanese intervention remained elevated.

sections.title: Commodities
sections.content:
Commodities were dominated by the unwinding of the Middle East risk premium as tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz normalised. Brent crude traded near unchanged on the week, returning to pre-war levels with support emerging ahead of **$70**, as recovering supply flows were largely priced in and talk of a growing glut built, with Iran reporting over **40 million** barrels shipped since the naval blockade was lifted and Russian exports at record highs.

Gold rallied, briefly trading above **$4,100** after rebounding from an intraday low near **$3,960**, as Fed Chair Warsh eased speculation over rate hikes and lower energy prices and a soft jobs report reduced tightening odds.

The pullback in oil eased the inflation impulse and softened real-yield pressure, supporting precious metals and improving the cross-asset backdrop for equities and duration.

sections.title: Fixed Income
sections.content:
Sovereign yields rose over the week even as they fell on jobs day, leaving the curve steeper. The 10-year UST yield climbed roughly **10bp** on the week to around **4.49%**, its largest weekly gain since mid-May and ending a three-week decline, while the 2-year eased on Thursday to about **4.13%** as the light payrolls print reduced Fed hike expectations.

The soft labour data reinforced the view that the Fed will keep rates on hold for the remainder of 2026 rather than tighten, with markets pricing a steady policy path amid easing energy-driven inflation risks.

Credit tone stayed constructive as risk sentiment improved, with IG resilient and HY supported by the equity rally; Edward Jones reiterated a preference for equities over bonds and for US large- and mid-cap alongside emerging-market stocks into the second half.
